chapters.title: America must face its flaws before it can heal
chapters.text:
One of the most urgent problems in America is that the country refuses to face the obvious truth about how deep racial segregation still runs. A lot of white Americans still act as though they own the country and get to decide who belongs in it. They treat the legacy of slavery as a long-closed chapter. When Black Americans raise complaints, they are brushed off as playing the victim. White Americans rarely admit that being white comes with built-in advantages.

Healing only starts when wounds are acknowledged and forgiveness becomes possible.

White privilege is so woven into daily life that many white Americans do not even see it. Black Americans often fear the police, even when they have done nothing wrong. They walk through their days with that fear sitting in their chest, knowing a jumpy officer could shoot them and likely face no consequences. As long as America refuses to face the harm it has done to Black people, segregation will not end. You cannot break a bad habit when the person practicing it cannot see anything wrong.

Freeing Black Americans is a project that has to involve both white and Black people. A nation with real opportunity and no segregation will produce more for everyone. America has to recognize the pain of the African American community, because only then is there a way forward. We need to dig into the history that taught us these lessons and build a better America for what comes next.

chapters.title: The damage done by America's political illusions
chapters.text:
In 1963, a group of Black student activists from Howard University organized a conference on the role Black writers should play in the civil rights movement. They invited James Baldwin, playwrights Lorraine Hansberry and Ossie Davis, and novelists John O. Killens and Ralph Ellison. Although young, the students had already taken beatings from the South for standing against segregation. Lunch counters were split by race, and Black people were still blocked from registering to vote. Many of them had been beaten or chased by the Ku Klux Klan and by racist sheriffs. From childhood, these students had lived inside the civil rights movement, carrying trauma that almost crushed them in a region that did not want to change and was soaked in segregation.

When Baldwin took the stage, he talked about the struggles Black men faced in America. For him, the fight for freedom meant confronting a tricky and brutal system held up by the privileged and the unapologetic. He tore apart America's old stories and pushed the audience to look straight at the country as it really was. America had been built on lies, and Baldwin believed Black people had to throw those lies out to break free.

For centuries, Black people were wrongly labeled as inferior and treated as less. Those stereotypes are still around, painting Black people as lazy, dishonest, sexually out of control, violent, and dependent on government help. Baldwin challenged the students to question those stories, because lies of that kind could destroy their sense of self. If they accepted the lies, they would feed the system that lets America dodge the truth about its racial violence and its fallout around the world.

Honoring Black history is a lesson the next generation needs.

To build a new America, Baldwin argued, the country has to admit the racist inheritance of its past and present, and how that inheritance has shaped every American's identity.

chapters.title: Finding an identity under the weight of oppression
chapters.text:
On September 4, 1957, Herman Counts, a theology professor, decided to test the new anti-segregation law the government had just passed. He dropped his daughter Dorothy at Harding High School, a hostile place where she was about to become the first Black student among a student body that was loudly against integration. Dorothy walked toward the school with her head held high. As she moved forward, white students screamed and cursed at her. They threw things and spat on her new dress. The police did nothing to protect her, and no school official stepped in to calm the crowd or guide her inside.

Dorothy's experience was horrible, but it was not unique. Over the next sixteen years, Baldwin watched American racism grow more savage. Black youths faced violence in both the South and the North. Countless people were targeted with violence, and nonviolent activists were attacked and killed. People who only wanted to vote were murdered and dumped into the Mississippi River. Overwhelmed by the racial climate, Baldwin moved to France to protect his mental health. Even in France, though, he discovered that escaping America's shadow was harder than he thought.

The past shapes the future, and it has to be faced honestly.

Black people are not just products of social forces. Each person has the right to define their own identity. No matter what labels America pins on him, Baldwin believed he was the one writing the final story of who he was, as a person and as a Black man.

What Black Americans go through today echoes the struggles Baldwin and earlier generations faced when the Black liberation movement seemed to be falling apart. The powerful keep silencing the people, and unless we hold on to history, we are going to keep failing.

A quick statistic. According to the New York Civil Liberties Union, 88 percent of police stops in New York City in 2018 involved Black and Latinx people, while only 10 percent involved white people.

chapters.title: King's commitment to nonviolence is questioned
chapters.text:
Baldwin met Martin Luther King Jr. at a fundraiser on March 16, 1968. King had always pushed a liberal, peaceful approach to ending segregation, but patience for the liberal method was running out. People were starting to doubt his strategies, because the strategies did not match the aggressive action that seemed needed to break through the deep lies of America. Watching King that night, Baldwin realized that King could not deliver the urgent change Black Americans needed without taking the radical steps that some considered essential to freedom.

As long as there are people willing to enable oppression, there will be people willing to resist it.

King spoke at length about the weight of desegregation. He felt the liberation movement was slowly losing, because America was a fundamentally racist country.

In the days before King was killed at the Lorraine Motel, Baldwin had joined him for a fundraiser in New York. Lacking a proper dark suit, Baldwin had rushed to buy one and had it altered. Two weeks later, he wore that same suit to King's funeral. King's assassination was a signal from the white ruling class that they were not ready for desegregation.

When suffering crossed a line, Black people knew they had to stand and fight back. Armed members of the Black Panthers walked into the California State Capitol in Sacramento to protest the Mulford Act, a bill aimed at limiting the open carry of firearms. The bill was a direct response to the Panthers' armed monitoring of police activity in Oakland. The Panthers' chairman, Bobby Seale, read out Executive Mandate Number One. He insisted that Black people recognize the racist, systemic law designed to give the police more power over Black communities.

Hope gets invented every day, as James Baldwin once put it.

Look at the country now, and those same kinds of policies are still being used to hold Black people down. If America is going to be redeemed, its people have to commit completely, with every part of themselves, and with a generous share of compassion.

chapters.title: Taking on America's myths
chapters.text:
King's death pushed Baldwin into a deep depression. In 1969, his despair drove him to try to take his own life, and he ended up in the hospital. During that time, he told close friends that he was balancing on the edge, struggling to absorb the loss of the friends he had buried. King's death also shook the civil rights movement. Many agreed with Baldwin that King's death marked the end of the Black Liberation Movement.

Baldwin stepped away for a while and came back with a manuscript he had no plan to publish. His brother David, though, took the manuscript and handed it to his editor at Dial Press. Within a few weeks, the book was on shelves under the title No Name in the Streets.

The book laid out how Baldwin was wrestling with a sense of surrender as friends died and the movement he had given so much to fell apart. His attempt to pick up the pieces of his life became a portrait of America itself. Like the country, Baldwin had watched oppression and been unable to do enough to stop it. His life and the life of the country were both marked by trauma and the lies built to cover that trauma. In the end, every part of life, including his own suffering, had been turned into a political weapon.

Today, plenty of white Americans openly back racism and argue that Black people should be grateful for whatever scraps they are given. Baldwin pushed back on that view, but those people cannot see the truth, because they refuse to face or understand what Black communities deal with. They benefit from systemic racism and ignore real history, holding on to a vision of a permanently white America. For them, America has to stay white forever, and the election of Donald Trump in 2016 is a sign that the fight for freedom is far from over.

Oppressors thrive when the people they are crushing stay quiet.

chapters.title: America has to do better in the present
chapters.text:
Over the decades, America has made some progress as the obvious walls of segregation began to crack. Black people started climbing into political roles and the Black middle class grew, both signs of improvement compared to the country of the 1960s. More women entered the workforce, and sexualities that had been condemned became more accepted. The march toward full freedom, though, stalled. White oppressors made their anger clear, and instead of practicing open racism, they switched to systemic discrimination. That quieter approach let them hide behind legal policy and frustrate the Black community while still painting Black people as the villains.

The struggles Baldwin and King faced as the civil rights movement was beaten back have not been resolved. Despite landmark laws like the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965, America has not fully embraced change. The heroic and painful efforts of the Black Freedom Movement to remake the country are slowly being undone. Black Americans still sit at the bottom of the social ladder, and police brutality keeps tearing through their communities. To a degree, the Black Freedom Movement did change America, but a great deal of work is still ahead.

For Black Americans, the present is still hard. If people stay committed to what they believe, things can change. They have to keep believing that the world can still be made better, and they have to hold on to hope, even when the odds look thin.

If change is going to come to America, progressive candidates have to win elections.

Trump's arrival gives Black Americans a choice. They can put up with discriminatory policies by re-electing him, or they can look for a candidate seen as an improvement, or they can try to break the political system that has kept them on the margins. If voters choose Trump or someone seen as a small step up, they should expect harder times. If they come together and pick someone who shares their experiences, they might finally get to help birth a new America.

Ignorance, when it is tied to power, becomes the most dangerous enemy of justice. That line is from James Baldwin.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
America's long history of racism stretches back centuries, leaving the Black community to deal with constant segregation and brutal treatment just because of the color of their skin. In the era of the slave trade, millions of Africans were thrown away, ripped from their lands, and shipped to a foreign country. After the slave trade ended, they still had no home to go back to, so they had to stay, only to be met with more racism. Many white Americans believed, and some still do, that Black people are not fully human and do not deserve respect.

Through the years, the Black community has pushed back and fought to make the point that they are equally human, no matter their skin color. James Baldwin, who lived through the worst parts of white supremacy, told the stories of Black Americans. His fights for the people, and his moments of being worn down and beaten, showed the world how long Black people have suffered.

Progress has been made, but America today still wrestles with racism and segregation. Leaders like former President Donald Trump have revived echoes of the country's discriminatory past. Black Americans keep getting killed for little or no reason, and racism keeps growing. With determination and unity inside the Black community, things can change for the better.

A small thing to try.

Teach yourself by reading history books on racism and Black history. It will help you understand the Black experience and see why the call for a better society keeps coming. Stand up against racism whenever you see it, and help shape a better society around you.
